question: Why should you never use MultipartFile.getOriginalFilename() directly as the stored filename?
options: The original filename may contain Unicode characters that crash the filesystem
An attacker can supply a filename like '../../etc/passwd' to write files outside the upload directory
Spring truncates filenames longer than 64 characters
getOriginalFilename() returns null for files over 1 MB

question: A user uploads a file with Content-Type: image/jpeg, but the actual bytes are a .exe file. Which validation approach detects this?
options: Checking file.getContentType() — it reflects the true file type
Checking file.getOriginalFilename() extension — .jpg extension means it's an image
Using a library like Apache Tika to sniff the actual bytes for a MIME signature
Spring Boot automatically validates MIME types and rejects mismatches

question: What is the purpose of the Content-Disposition: inline header when serving a file download?
options: It tells the browser to cache the file for 30 days
It tells the browser to render the file in-page (image, PDF) rather than prompting a download
It prevents the file from being served over HTTP without HTTPS
It sets the file size so the browser can show a progress bar

question: You store uploaded files on the server's local disk. The app is deployed to two load-balanced instances. What goes wrong?
options: Spring's multipart parser fails when multiple instances are running
Files uploaded to instance A are not visible to instance B — users get 404 on the second request
The OS file system locks files so only one instance can write at a time
Nothing — local disk storage works fine across multiple instances

question: Which Spring Boot configuration limits the maximum size of a single uploaded file to 10 MB?
options: spring.servlet.multipart.max-request-size=10MB
spring.mvc.max-upload-size=10MB
spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size=10MB
server.tomcat.max-part-size=10MB
